title: Ron And Tammy

jokes.text: And I've come to the conclusion that I've completely wasted mine.
jokes.setup: Old Gus giving what appears to be a sentimental retirement speech
jokes.punchline: Unexpected confession of life regret during celebration
jokes.explanation: Dark turn in ceremonial speech. Economy is excellent - devastating self-assessment in few words.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about my time here, and I want to say something from the heart.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: This job, this town, these people... they've meant everything to me.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: But I gotta be honest with you all. I've wasted my whole life.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: I should've traveled. I should've learned to dance. I should've told my ex-wife I was sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: Instead, I just sat around complaining about everything.

jokes.text: And just for the record, I never ever liked being called old Gus. I didn't understand it when I was in my 20's and I sure hate it now.
jokes.setup: Character known as 'Old Gus' for 50 years
jokes.punchline: He's always hated the nickname, even when young
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic - hating being called 'old' even in his 20s. Character-specific revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: They've called me 'Old Gus' for fifty years.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: I've always hated it, even when I was young.

jokes.text: So go to hell, every single one of you. Screw Pawnee and screw your damn shoes.
jokes.setup: Retirement speech taking increasingly dark turns
jokes.punchline: Complete meltdown ending with cursing the shoes he's been shining
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation capping the speech. Specific detail about shoes shows character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: I've been shining shoes in this town for forty-seven years. It's been an honor to serve you all.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: But you know what? Nobody appreciates it. Nobody says thank you. You just walk in, drop your shoes like I'm some kind of servant.

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: My wife left me. My kids won't call. And for what? For what?!

dialogue.speaker: Old Gus
dialogue.line: These shoes! These cursed shoes! I've wasted my entire life on these shoes! To hell with these shoes! To hell with all of you!

jokes.text: Old Gus is classic.
jokes.setup: Old Gus just delivered an extremely hostile retirement speech
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement treating the outburst as charming
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan reaction. Maximum economy - two words capture absurd understatement.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, that was... spirited!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I think he called us all 'incompetent weasels.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But in a fun way!

jokes.text: April, stop that. Who are you texting? / You. / She's texting me.
jokes.setup: Leslie catching April texting during meeting
jokes.punchline: April is texting Andy who's sitting right there
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for April's antisocial nature. Absurd but totally believable for her.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, are you texting during the meeting?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Then who are you texting?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy's sitting right there!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's faster than talking to her.

jokes.text: Sorry you're bored.
jokes.setup: April caught texting during meeting
jokes.punchline: No shame, just matter-of-fact apology for being bored
jokes.explanation: Pure April - zero attempt to hide her disinterest. Character-perfect response.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I was texting. I'm bored.

jokes.text: Would you rather be able to fly or speak fluent French? Donna, go. / French.
jokes.setup: Leslie leaves Tom in charge to keep meeting productive
jokes.punchline: Tom immediately derails into would-you-rather questions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom move - immediately abandoning responsibility for frivolous games. Character-driven humor.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, I'm leaving you in charge. Keep this meeting productive.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You got it, Leslie. I'm on it.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay everyone, would you rather fight one horse-sized duck or a hundred duck-sized horses?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Tom, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Keeping things productive. Now answer the question.

jokes.text: Damn it! The library?
jokes.setup: Another department filed for lot 48
jokes.punchline: Leslie's disproportionate horror that it's the library specifically
jokes.explanation: Sets up Leslie's irrational hatred of libraries. Good character-specific reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The library? They filed for the library? No, no, no. Not the library. Anyone but the library. We could lose the rec center, the animal shelter, the old folks home—I mean, those would be tragedies, obviously—but the library? The library is sacred. The library is where knowledge lives. Where dreams are born. Where I lost my virginity!

jokes.text: Pawnee's Library Department is the most diabolical, ruthless bunch of bureaucrats I've ever seen. They're like a biker gang. But instead of shotguns and crystal meth, they use political savvy. And shushing.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing the library as threatening
jokes.punchline: Comparing librarians to bikers, but their weapons are shushing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation with perfect misdirection. 'Shushing' as weapon is inspired absurdist detail.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The library is a scary place. Librarians are like bikers, except instead of leather jackets and motorcycles, their weapons are the power of shushing.

jokes.text: News flash, we're screwed. We got a big problem with the library. Punk ass book jockeys.
jokes.setup: Tom joining the library hatred
jokes.punchline: 'Punk ass book jockeys' as an insult
jokes.explanation: 'Book jockeys' is a clever spin on 'disc jockeys.' Tom's attempt to sound tough is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Punk ass book jockeys.

jokes.text: It's the worst group of people ever assembled. They're mean, conniving, rude, and well read, which makes them very dangerous.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing terrible traits of librarians
jokes.punchline: 'Well read' as the most dangerous quality
jokes.explanation: Subverts expectation by making reading sound threatening. Classic Leslie overreaction.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Librarians are the worst. They're always shushing people, they smell like old books, and they have an unhealthy relationship with due dates.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But the most dangerous quality a librarian can have? Being 'well read.' Because then they actually know things, and they'll correct you in front of everyone.

jokes.text: I actually think a library would be nice that close to my house. But I'm not about to say that in there.
jokes.setup: Everyone expressing hatred of libraries
jokes.punchline: Ann secretly thinks a library would be nice but won't admit it
jokes.explanation: Ann as voice of reason, afraid to contradict group hysteria. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I mean, obviously libraries are terrible and a complete waste of taxpayer money.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: But... like, hypothetically... if someone wanted to go to a library and just read books in a quiet place for a few hours, that wouldn't be the worst thing in the world.

jokes.text: Ron's ex-wife? / That's terrific. Or is that awful? / He hates her, but he knows her. / Everything's okay. Or is it just the same? / You're thinking out loud again. / Am I? I am.
jokes.setup: Leslie processing information about Tammy
jokes.punchline: Leslie's stream-of-consciousness panic, getting called out for it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie neurotic processing. Ann's gentle correction shows their friendship dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tammy is coming to Pawnee? Tammy Two or Tammy One? Please say Tammy Two. Oh my God, what if it's both Tammys? What if they've formed some kind of Tammy alliance against me? That's insane. That's actually insane. I need to call Tom. I need to call everyone. I need to move to Canada.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're spiraling.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm not spiraling, you're spiraling!

jokes.text: Of course, that bitch of an ex-wife is working for the library now. That is perfect. The worst person in the world working at the worst place in the world.
jokes.setup: Ron learning Tammy works at the library
jokes.punchline: Perfect alignment of his two greatest hatreds
jokes.explanation: Ron's worldview perfectly aligned - his worst enemy at his least favorite institution.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She works at the library? The library? That's the perfect alignment of my two greatest hatreds.

jokes.text: Does she have any weaknesses? / What do you mean no? Everybody has one. / Not machines.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking about Tammy's weaknesses
jokes.punchline: Ron calling Tammy a machine, not human
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline structure. Ron's dehumanizing of Tammy is both funny and revealing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What are Tammy's weaknesses?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She's not human. She's a machine. Machines don't have weaknesses.

jokes.text: I honestly believe that she was programmed by someone from the future to come back and destroy all happiness.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining Tammy isn't human
jokes.punchline: Elaborate Terminator-style origin story for his ex-wife
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from 'machine' to full sci-fi backstory. Specific mission 'destroy happiness' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy isn't human. She was built in a secret government lab in 1987 as the perfect weapon. She has a titanium skeleton, hydraulic muscles, and a heart made of ice. They sent her back in time to destroy my life, and she succeeded. She's a Terminator, but worse—she's a Taminator.

jokes.text: On my death bed, my final wish is to have my ex-wives rushed to my side, so I can use my dying breath to tell them both to go to hell, one last time.
jokes.setup: Ron expressing hatred for his ex-wives
jokes.punchline: Elaborate deathbed revenge fantasy
jokes.explanation: Dark but hilarious commitment to hatred. The specificity and dedication make it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have a fantasy where each of my ex-wives is on her deathbed, and I get to say goodbye. So I show up and say, 'Hey.' And she says, 'Ron, I'm so sorry for everything I did wrong.' And I say, 'I know,' and I leave. That's the fantasy.

jokes.text: Would I get married again? Absolutely. If you don't believe in love, what's the point of living?
jokes.setup: Ron just expressed intense hatred for both ex-wives
jokes.punchline: Immediately segueing into romantic optimism
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic juxtaposition. Ron's contradictory nature perfectly captured in consecutive beats.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have nothing but contempt for my first two wives. But I have a good feeling about wife number three.

jokes.text: And in case something bad goes down, I wore my sharpest rings. This one will tear you up.
jokes.setup: Leslie preparing for confrontation with librarians
jokes.punchline: Leslie treating jewelry as weapons for library visit
jokes.explanation: Leslie's over-preparation for mundane situation. Character-perfect paranoia about libraries.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going into the library. I may not come out alive. But if I don't, tell my story. Tell them I died doing what I loved.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Also, I brought weapons. Jewelry weapons. Pointy earrings, a sharp brooch. If things get ugly, I'm going in swinging.

jokes.text: You have a lot of nerve showing your face here. / Excuse me? / You have overdue book fees totaling 3 dollars, Missy.
jokes.setup: Aggressive confrontation at library
jokes.punchline: The 'crime' is just $3 in overdue fees
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - builds up dramatic confrontation for tiny infraction. Anticlimax works.

dialogue.speaker: Library employee
dialogue.line: You owe $3 in overdue fees.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Three dollars? That's it? I thought I was being arrested for murder!

dialogue.speaker: Library employee
dialogue.line: No, just the fees.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh thank God. I was worried for nothing.

jokes.text: That is so typical. I should've known you'd use a low blow, dirty pool, B.S. move like that. That's why everybody hates the library. Here's your 3 dollars. See you in hell.
jokes.setup: Minor overdue fee revelation
jokes.punchline: Leslie treating $3 fee as declaration of war
jokes.explanation: Leslie's disproportionate response perfect for character. 'See you in hell' over library fees is great.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A three dollar fee? THREE DOLLARS? Do you know what this means?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It means you owe the library three dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It means war, Ben. It means war.

jokes.text: I was just kidding, dear God. You did have 3 dollars worth of fines, but I cleared them.
jokes.setup: Leslie's explosive reaction to library confrontation
jokes.punchline: Library employee was joking, reveals herself as reasonable
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of tension. Shows Leslie's paranoia about libraries is unfounded.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: You can't just joke about that! This is a library! These are books!

dialogue.speaker: Library employee
dialogue.line: I was just messing with you. I'm actually totally fine with it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Oh. Well... that's... I may have overreacted.

jokes.text: I know this is a trap but I don't know how.
jokes.setup: Tammy being unexpectedly nice
jokes.punchline: Leslie's inability to accept kindness from librarian
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie paranoia. Economical line that captures her worldview perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: Leslie, I think you're doing a great job.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? Why would you say that? What do you want? Are you dying? Did you poison me?

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to be nice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, stop it. It's creepy and it's making me very uncomfortable.

jokes.text: I swear, I don't have cloven feet.
jokes.setup: Tammy defending herself against Ron's characterization
jokes.punchline: Specific denial of having devil hooves
jokes.explanation: Funny specific detail suggesting Ron's described her as literally demonic.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: I do not have devil hooves. These are normal feet.

jokes.text: Shine, shine, shine your shoes? What do you say, sir? Ma'am, shoeshine? I won't look up your skirt.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to be a professional shoeshine person
jokes.punchline: Immediately making inappropriate comment about not looking up skirts
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy - trying to be reassuring but making things worse. Character-perfect inappropriate comment.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna be a professional shoeshine person. I promise I won't look up your skirt.

jokes.text: Just trying the rat race. Chasing the cheese. Racing the rats. To get the cheese.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining his work situation
jokes.punchline: Completely mangling the 'rat race' metaphor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy malapropism. His confusion of common sayings is character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not running in the rat race, I'm running in the rat... marathon. Which is longer and harder, so I'm winning.

jokes.text: It is definitely due to... the economy. I've been hearing a lot of people say that a lot, about a lot of stuff.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining lack of customers
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's just parroting what others say about the economy
jokes.explanation: Andy's honest admission of not understanding but repeating buzzwords. Character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, the economy is really bad right now. People aren't spending money. It's a tough market out there.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Honestly, I have no idea what I'm talking about. I just hear people say that stuff and I repeat it.

jokes.text: I'm more than a friend of Ann's. / Not for long. / I'm sorry? / I don't know.
jokes.setup: Mark asserting relationship with Ann
jokes.punchline: Andy's casual threat followed by immediate backtrack
jokes.explanation: Andy's impulsive threat followed by confusion at his own words. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Ann and I are together now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, I'm gonna punch you in the face!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Just kidding. You seem like a good guy.

jokes.text: When we were dating, the only job he had was sending audition tapes to Survivor and Deal or no deal.
jokes.setup: Ann explaining Andy's previous employment
jokes.punchline: Andy's 'job' was applying to reality TV shows
jokes.explanation: Perfect insight into Andy's delusional career aspirations. Character-revealing detail.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: His job was applying to reality TV shows.

jokes.text: Hi, my name is Andy Dwyer, and I would be a perfect contestant for deal or no deal.
jokes.setup: Flashback to Andy's audition tape
jokes.punchline: Andy's overconfident audition for a luck-based game show
jokes.explanation: Andy's delusion about being 'perfect' for a random game. Shows his lack of self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Andy Dwyer, and I'm here to audition for Wheel of Fortune. I've been preparing for this my whole life. I'm incredibly lucky. One time I found a twenty dollar bill in my jacket pocket. I didn't even know it was there. That's luck. I'm basically a luck machine.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've also been studying the wheel. I know all the letters. A, B, C, D, E... I could go on, but I don't want to show all my cards before the audition. I'm also very good at guessing. My guess right now is that this audition is going great.

jokes.text: And off to the side, a lovely sitting area for kids with asthma to watch other kids play.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing her ideal park
jokes.punchline: Specific accommodation for kids who can't participate
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie over-consideration. Dark humor in segregating sick kids but presented innocently.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: My ideal park would have a special area for kids who can't participate in normal activities. We'd call it the 'Participation Trophy Garden.'

jokes.text: If I'd had a park like that when I was growing up, I probably wouldn't have gone through such a prolonged mall slut phase.
jokes.setup: Leslie reflecting on her childhood
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to being a 'mall slut'
jokes.explanation: Unexpected revelation delivered matter-of-factly. 'Mall slut' is specific and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I was a mall slut in the '90s.

jokes.text: What's it like to stare into the eye of Satan's butt hole?
jokes.setup: Tom asking about Leslie's meeting with Tammy
jokes.punchline: Elaborate anatomical insult combining religious and scatological imagery
jokes.explanation: Creative insult with vivid imagery. Tom's crude humor style. Memorable construction.

jokes.text: You slept with Brendanawicz?
jokes.setup: Leslie mentioning sleeping with Mark
jokes.punchline: Tom's shocked reaction and use of Mark's last name
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat. Using formal last name shows Tom's shock at the revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You slept with Mark Brendanawicz?!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mark Brendanawicz! That's like sleeping with a piece of cardboard that got sad!

jokes.text: We are oil and water. Or oil and TNT and C-4 and a detonator and a butane torch.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining incompatibility with Tammy
jokes.punchline: Escalating from simple metaphor to elaborate explosive scenario
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure. Ron's paranoid specificity about explosives is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy and I are like oil and water.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: We don't mix. We repel each other.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: If you put us in a container together, the container will explode.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: The explosion will be so powerful it will take out a city block.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: And then birds will flee in terror.

jokes.text: You opened the gates. To crazy town.
jokes.setup: Donna warning Leslie about reuniting Ron and Tammy
jokes.punchline: 'Crazy town' as literal destination with gates
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic pause and delivery. Treating crazy town as real place is effective.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Leslie, you cannot reunite Ron and Tammy. That is crazy town.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What do you mean? It'll be fine.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: No. Crazy town. Population: you.

jokes.text: You've aged horribly. / You... son of a bitch.
jokes.setup: Ron and Tammy's reunion after years apart
jokes.punchline: Immediate mutual insults instead of pleasantries
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of reunion expectations. Economical exchange showing their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy. You look exactly the same.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: So do you. Except fatter.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's woodworking dust, not fat.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: Sure it is.

jokes.text: Who set the bed on fire?
jokes.setup: Ron and Tammy fighting in background
jokes.punchline: Casual question about bed being on fire
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - implies destruction without showing it. Casual delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Why is the bed on fire?

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: Because you touch yourself at night.

jokes.text: When you meddle in someone's personal life, it's just so... rewarding.
jokes.setup: Leslie observing aftermath of Ron/Tammy reunion chaos
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic satisfaction despite obvious disaster
jokes.explanation: Leslie's inability to admit mistakes even when evidence is overwhelming. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, Ron and Tammy Two got back together, which is great for them, terrible for literally everyone else, but I'm choosing to see the silver lining here, which is that at least they'll be destroying each other's lives instead of ours.

jokes.text: That is a beautiful sweater vest. You look like you could use $20. Am I right? 'Cause you're a kid and kids always need money.
jokes.setup: Ron in unusually good mood after sex
jokes.punchline: Giving April money based on stereotypical assumption about kids
jokes.explanation: Ron's post-sex generosity showing his transformed mood. Character-revealing moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: April, I'm in an excellent mood. Here's twenty dollars.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why are you giving me money?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Because you're a child, and children like money for candy and... whatever it is you people do.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not a child, Ron. I'm a grown woman.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Well, you're certainly not acting like one.

jokes.text: When Tiger Woods feels invincible, he wears a red shirt and black pants. Ron wears the same thing after he had sex.
jokes.setup: Tiger Woods' confidence clothes ritual
jokes.punchline: Ron has same ritual but for different reason
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parallel construction. Specific sports reference elevated by character observation.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Tiger Woods wears red on Sundays because it's his power color. It gives him confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have the same ritual, but for a different reason. I wear red on Sundays so people know to stay away from me.

jokes.text: But this stock photo, I bought at a framing store, isn't real.
jokes.setup: Ron talking about liking breakfast food
jokes.punchline: Admitting his 'family photo' is fake stock photography
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron deadpan delivery. Reveals his loneliness while maintaining stoic facade.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I like breakfast food. I like hash browns, I like wheat toast, I like bacon. Why do I have to choose? I want all the breakfast foods.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That family photo on my desk? Stock photography. I have no children and I've never been to the Grand Canyon.

jokes.text: A naked Tammy made me breakfast this morning. I should've taken a picture of it.
jokes.setup: Ron describing his morning
jokes.punchline: Regretting not photographing naked Tammy making breakfast
jokes.explanation: Ron's practical regret about not documenting the moment. Character-appropriate response.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I woke up this morning and Tammy was making breakfast naked. I should have photographed it.

jokes.text: That's a tough one. Break up with her and tell her to go out with me.
jokes.setup: Andy asking for relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Tom's 'advice' is purely self-serving
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom opportunism. Transparent self-interest disguised as advice.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Tom, I need some relationship advice. April's been distant lately.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, here's what you do. You buy her something expensive. Very expensive. Like, from my store.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's not relationship advice, that's a sales pitch.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, Andy, I'm just saying — if you want to fix things with April, you gotta show her you care. And the best way to do that is to give me money. I mean, give her a gift. From Rent-A-Swag.

jokes.text: I've never taken the high road. But I tell other people to. 'Cause then there's more room for me on the low road.
jokes.setup: Tom giving Andy advice about taking the high road
jokes.punchline: Revealing his strategy is to clear the low road for himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character revelation. Perfect logical structure and cynical wisdom. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You gotta take the high road, Andy. Let other people bring you down to their level.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, that makes sense.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That way, the low road is completely clear for me.

jokes.text: Leslie, I can't thank you enough for sticking your nose where it didn't belong.
jokes.setup: Leslie expecting gratitude for helping
jokes.punchline: Ron's sarcastic thanks for meddling
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic delivery. Ron acknowledging Leslie's interference with mock gratitude.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're welcome, Ron. I'm glad I could help.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Thank you, Leslie. Thank you for your meddling. It means so much to me that you can't respect boundaries or let people handle their own problems. I really appreciate you inserting yourself into my personal affairs without being asked.

jokes.text: I didn't know where my flesh stopped and hers began.
jokes.setup: Ron sharing intimate details
jokes.punchline: Overly graphic description of sex
jokes.explanation: Ron's uncharacteristic oversharing shows how Tammy affects him. Makes audience uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't like to brag, but I'm excellent in bed. I'm a heavy sleeper.

jokes.text: It's like doing peyote and sneezing slowly for six hours.
jokes.setup: Ron describing sex with Tammy
jokes.punchline: Bizarre drug/bodily function comparison
jokes.explanation: Completely original and bizarre comparison. Memorable and quotable absurdist imagery.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's like doing heroin, except instead of a needle in your arm, it's a Tammy in your soul.

jokes.text: She knows her way around a penis.
jokes.setup: Ron continuing graphic sexual details
jokes.punchline: Blunt anatomical expertise assessment
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor from oversharing. Less original than previous lines but serves character arc.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I know what I'm talking about. I've been around the block.

jokes.text: I can't even tell you how many of my exes' weddings I've been to.
jokes.setup: Leslie promoting being friends with exes
jokes.punchline: Revealing she's been to multiple ex-boyfriends' weddings
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie over-friendliness. Reveals her pattern of failed relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I think it's great to be friends with your exes. I mean, why wouldn't you want to maintain those relationships?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've been to like, what, five of my exes' weddings? Six?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I caught the bouquet at two of them.

jokes.text: Three fistfuls of hair? That's not even possible.
jokes.setup: Someone reading Ron and Tammy's divorce papers
jokes.punchline: Physical impossibility of the described violence
jokes.explanation: Good observation about absurd violence described in legal documents.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: These divorce papers say Tammy tried to kill me by wrapping me in a carpet and rolling me off a cliff.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Ron, that's... how is that even physically possible?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: That's what the judge said.

jokes.text: Well, I'm too classy to say I told you so, donna. So I wrote it on a post it.
jokes.setup: April claiming to be too classy for 'I told you so'
jokes.punchline: Writing it down instead as workaround
jokes.explanation: Perfect April logic - finding loophole in social convention. Character-perfect petty satisfaction.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm not the type of person who says 'I told you so.' I'm way too classy for that.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: But I do write it down. So I have a record of it.

jokes.text: But at the end of the day what does it matter if the lot becomes a park or a museum or a mega church? / Or a library. / Nobody said library.
jokes.setup: Ron listing random possibilities for the lot
jokes.punchline: Someone adding library; Ron denying he mentioned it
jokes.explanation: Good setup revealing Ron's subconscious. His denial shows guilty conscience.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: We could put in a big parking lot, or a small parking lot, or a medium parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What about a library?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I never said that.

jokes.text: More sex.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking what Ron's trading the lot for
jokes.punchline: Blunt two-word admission
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy - devastating honesty in two words. Perfect deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Scotch.

jokes.text: Les, there are two kinds of women in this world. There are women who work hard and stress out about doing the right thing and then there are women who are cool.
jokes.setup: Tammy explaining women's categories
jokes.punchline: Insulting Leslie while positioning herself as superior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tammy manipulation - backhanded insult disguised as wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: There are three kinds of women: the hot ones, the smart ones, and the ones who are neither. Leslie is the third kind.

jokes.text: You could either be a Cleopatra or you could be an Eleanor Roosevelt. / What kind of lunatic would wanna be Cleopatra over Eleanor Roosevelt?
jokes.setup: Tammy offering historical role model choice
jokes.punchline: Leslie's horror at choosing seductive over virtuous
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Leslie's priorities. Historical references show intelligence.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: You can be either a seductive role model or a virtuous role model.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Those are my only two options? Seductive or virtuous? Why can't I be both? Why can't I be neither? Why does it have to be so binary?

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: Choose.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Virtuous! Virtuous, obviously virtuous!

jokes.text: Haven't you ever messed with a man's head just to see what you could get him to do for you? We do it all the time in the library department.
jokes.setup: Tammy explaining manipulation tactics
jokes.punchline: Revealing it's standard practice in libraries
jokes.explanation: Confirms Leslie's paranoia about librarians being evil. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: The key to manipulation is patience and understanding human weakness. You identify what someone wants, then you control the supply.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: It's basic psychology. Works every time.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: That's... that's horrifying.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: It's standard library procedure. The late fees, the hold times, the 'lost' reservation requests. We invented the game.

jokes.text: I would never work at the library department.
jokes.setup: Tammy suggesting Leslie join the library
jokes.punchline: Leslie's absolute refusal despite career advantages
jokes.explanation: Leslie's moral inflexibility even when tempted. Shows character integrity.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: You should join the library. You'd make an excellent librarian.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. I would never work at the library. Never.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: But you love reading and organizing things.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I don't care if it's the perfect job. I'm not working at the library. The Parks Department is where I belong.

jokes.text: We're no longer government gals.
jokes.setup: Tammy previously bonding with Leslie as 'government gals'
jokes.punchline: Formally ending their alliance
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier bonding moment. Shows relationship deterioration.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: Leslie, I've enjoyed our time together as government gals, but I'm ending this alliance effective immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? Why? We were just bonding over bureaucratic procedures!

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: Precisely. It was getting too friendly. I have a reputation to maintain.

jokes.text: She made some really good points about libraries.
jokes.setup: Ron previously called library worst place in world
jokes.punchline: Now defending libraries due to sexual influence
jokes.explanation: Perfect character reversal showing Tammy's power over Ron. Betrays his core beliefs.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I may have been too harsh on the library. It turns out they have a very nice reading room, and the librarian has excellent posture.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, are you... attracted to the librarian?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm simply acknowledging that the library has redeeming qualities I previously overlooked.

jokes.text: Some people like libraries.
jokes.setup: Leslie's horror at Ron defending libraries
jokes.punchline: Ron's continued defense of his sworn enemy
jokes.explanation: Ron's complete capitulation to Tammy's influence. Shows his weakness around her.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, you're defending libraries?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have nothing against libraries. Librarians, on the other hand, are the bane of my existence. They promote the accumulation and retention of books and reading. I'm against that.

jokes.text: I'm so little.
jokes.setup: Ron admitting Tammy has all the power
jokes.punchline: Tough guy Ron describing himself as tiny and powerless
jokes.explanation: Perfect character destruction. Ron's vulnerability expressed in childlike terms is heartbreaking and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She has all the power. I am but a tiny, helpless man.

jokes.text: We would just end up naked. And I'd give her your lot and my house and God knows what else.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining why he can't break up with Tammy
jokes.punchline: Admitting he'd give away everything in sexual haze
jokes.explanation: Shows escalating stakes of Ron's addiction to Tammy. Good progression of consequences.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I can't break up with Tammy. She has this power over me. In a sexual haze, I'd give away everything - my land, my guns, my woodworking tools. Everything I've worked for my entire life.

jokes.text: You are Ron freakin' Swanson!
jokes.setup: Ron expressing powerlessness
jokes.punchline: Leslie's attempt to restore his confidence with his own name
jokes.explanation: Leslie's faith in Ron's reputation versus his current reality. Good friendship moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I feel powerless.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron Swanson, you are the most powerful man I know.

jokes.text: Not around her I'm not.
jokes.setup: Leslie reminding Ron of his strength
jokes.punchline: Ron's honest admission of being powerless around Tammy
jokes.explanation: Perfect honesty about his weakness. Shows self-awareness of his problem.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, you're the strongest man I know. You can do anything.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I appreciate that. But there is one thing I cannot do. I cannot say no to Tammy. She is my kryptonite.

jokes.text: "I let Mark nail me and we're still friends." / I never... I would never use those words.
jokes.setup: Ron quoting Leslie's earlier crude phrasing
jokes.punchline: Leslie's horror at the vulgar paraphrase
jokes.explanation: Good callback with crude interpretation. Leslie's propriety clashing with Ron's bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You said we need to 'get our shit together.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I did not say that!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Your exact words.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I would never say that in a professional setting!

jokes.text: I'm on a break. One of the many advantages of owning your own business.
jokes.setup: Andy not working at his shoeshine stand
jokes.punchline: Delusion about being a business owner
jokes.explanation: Andy's delusion about his employment status. Character-appropriate self-importance.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not at my shoeshine stand because I'm at a business conference. I'm expanding into the boot market.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You don't own a shoeshine stand.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude, I don't.

jokes.text: You know that's not your situation, right?
jokes.setup: Andy claiming to own his business
jokes.punchline: Tom's gentle reality check about Andy's employment
jokes.explanation: Tom's practical response to Andy's delusion. Good straight-man moment.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Andy, you don't own a business. You work for me.

jokes.text: If you had to choose, who would you choose? Right now, on the spot. / I'm not sure. Why don't you spin around for me.
jokes.setup: Andy asking Donna to judge between him and Mark
jokes.punchline: Donna treating it like a beauty contest with spinning
jokes.explanation: Donna's practical approach to judging men. Treats serious question with humor.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay Donna, I need you to be the judge. Who's more attractive, me or Mark?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Spin around.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Both of you, spin around. I need to see all angles.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Okay, you can stop. I've made my decision.

jokes.text: Ron brought you here to break up with me for him?
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to break up Ron and Tammy
jokes.punchline: Tammy's realization of the pathetic situation
jokes.explanation: Highlights the absurdity and cowardice of Ron's plan. Good cringe moment.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: You know what? You're right. I am too good for Ron. I deserve better than this pathetic man who works in government and makes no money.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy
dialogue.line: I'm leaving him.

jokes.text: She volunteered.
jokes.setup: Tammy's disbelief at the situation
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact correction about Leslie volunteering
jokes.explanation: Ron's deadpan response makes situation even more pathetic. Perfect delivery timing.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: You're telling me Leslie Knope volunteered to do this?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No. Leslie volunteered to do it. I'm making her do it.

jokes.text: Go to the sleaziest motel in town and wrap himself around me like a coiled snake.
jokes.setup: Tammy describing what Ron really wants
jokes.punchline: Vivid snake metaphor for sexual entanglement
jokes.explanation: Good vivid imagery and character insight. Shows Tammy's understanding of Ron's desire.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: Ron doesn't want a woman. Ron wants a snake. A big, long snake that he can keep in a cage and feed dead things to.

jokes.text: Is punching allowed on the high road?
jokes.setup: Andy trying to take Tom's advice about the high road
jokes.punchline: Asking if violence is permitted on the moral high ground
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to high road advice. Andy's logical but contradictory thinking is hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So you're saying I should take the high road?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's what I'm saying.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But is violence permitted on the moral high ground?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, Andy. No violence.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What about light violence?

jokes.text: Fine, I had one dream, but no, no.
jokes.setup: Tammy accusing Leslie of wanting Ron
jokes.punchline: Leslie's inadvertent admission of romantic dream
jokes.explanation: Leslie's unintentional confession undermines her position. Good character revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Tammy II
dialogue.line: You want Ron.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I do not want Ron!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I mean, not in real life. But I did have this dream once where we were on a sailboat and he made me laugh so hard I fell overboard and he dove in after me and—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to stop talking now.

jokes.text: If I'm not down in 5 min, it's only because I'm receiving a pleasure so intense...
jokes.setup: Ron asking Leslie to wait downstairs
jokes.punchline: Beginning to describe sexual pleasure before cutting off
jokes.explanation: Ron's continued oversharing pattern. Creates discomfort but shows his state of mind.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, wait downstairs.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Why? What's going on?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm about to experience something so pleasurable, so intense, so extraordinarily satisfying...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm going to sand this desk.

jokes.text: Andy and Ann's family shoeshine? I thought it had a nice ring to it.
jokes.setup: Andy creating promotional materials for his business
jokes.punchline: Naming business after himself and his ex-girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Andy's obliviousness to appropriateness. Classic lack of social awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm going to call it Treat Yo Self Enterprises.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, no. April and I broke up. I should probably change that.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: How about... Andy and April's Awesome Company?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, that's still weird. Okay, just Andy's Awesome Company.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? I'm keeping it. Andy and April's Awesome Company. That way she has to think about me every time she sees a business card.

jokes.text: Is part of your moustache missing? / Yes. / There's a push pin in your face. / Leave it in.
jokes.setup: Ron emerging from confrontation with Tammy
jokes.punchline: Multiple injuries including missing mustache and embedded push pin
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy escalation. Ron's stoic 'leave it in' is perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron! What happened to you?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't want to talk about it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Your mustache is gone!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I said I don't want to talk about it.

jokes.text: You didn't kill Tammy, did you? / I'm afraid she can't be killed.
jokes.setup: Leslie checking on Tammy's welfare
jokes.punchline: Ron treating Tammy as immortal supernatural being
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Tammy being inhuman. Ron's deadpan delivery of supernatural premise.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, how is Tammy doing? Is she okay?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She's fine. Tammy cannot be killed by conventional weapons.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She will live forever. I've made peace with it.

jokes.text: Tammy is... a mean person. / Come on, you can do better than that. / She's a great A bitch.
jokes.setup: Ron struggling to insult Tammy
jokes.punchline: Leslie coaching him to be more insulting
jokes.explanation: Leslie helping Ron process his feelings. Good character reversal - she's being the crude one.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, you need to really hurt her feelings. Tell her something that will really sting.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy, you are... a woman.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, no! That's not an insult, that's a compliment coming from you. Try again. Be mean!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Your hair looks like it was cut by someone who hates you.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: There we go! That's good! Keep going!

jokes.text: Every time she laughs, an angel dies. Even telemarketers avoid her. Her birth was payback for the sins of men.
jokes.setup: Ron building momentum insulting Tammy
jokes.punchline: Increasingly elaborate and supernatural insults
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating insults with theological implications. Each one more creative than the last.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tammy, you are a blight upon this department and everything it stands for.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You are a human wrecking ball of incompetence and poor life choices.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Your very presence drains the will to live from everyone around you like some kind of emotional vampire.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You are less useful than a screen door on a submarine, less welcome than a skunk at a garden party, and less relevant than a dial-up modem in the year 2012.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I wouldn't be surprised if you were actually a witch who had been cursed to live among us as punishment for some ancient crime against humanity.

jokes.text: But, you know, the worst thing about her, She works for the library.
jokes.setup: Ron listing Tammy's terrible supernatural qualities
jokes.punchline: Her job is somehow worse than being evil incarnate
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and character consistency. Working at library worse than being literally evil.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Her job is somehow worse than being evil incarnate.

tmdb_overview: Leslie is upset when she learns the library department wants to take over her lot. Making matters worse, the library is run by Ron's ex-wife Tammy, who still has a powerful emotional hold on him. Meanwhile, Andy gets a job in hopes of winning Ann back.
insight: Ron's terror of his ex-wife sustains 81 jokes across 22 minutes without mercy.
